title: Isaac Asimov's Hybrid Reading Guide
notes:
Your Path Forward 

I, Robot (1950) - (Short story collection) - done

Finish the Galactic Empire Trilogy: You've already started The Stars, Like Dust. It's best to complete the short Galactic Empire series to get those stories in the order they were published.
The Stars, Like Dust (1951) - done
The Currents of Space (1952) - done
Pebble in the Sky (1950) - done

Go Back to the Beginning (Chronologically): Now that you've finished the short stories from I, Robot and the Empire trilogy, you can move on to the chronologically first novels in the saga, which star detective Elijah Baley and his robotic partner, R. Daneel Olivaw. These stories build directly on the themes of the robot short stories.
The Caves of Steel (1954) - done
The Naked Sun (1957) - next/reading now
The Robots of Dawn (1983)
Robots and Empire (1985)

Read the Core Foundation Trilogy (Publication Order): With the Robot and Empire novels complete, you can now dive into the original Foundation series. Reading these in the order they were published preserves the carefully constructed plot and reveals.
Foundation (1951)
Foundation and Empire (1952)
Second Foundation (1953)

Complete the Saga: Finally, read the later sequels and prequels, which tie the entire universe together. Reading them now will make the connections to the earlier Robot novels much clearer.
Foundation's Edge (1982)
Foundation and Earth (1986)
Prelude to Foundation (1988)
Forward the Foundation (1993)

The Second Foundation Trilogy (Posthumous)
This trilogy was written by other authors with the authorization of the Asimov estate to continue the saga after Asimov's death. It is the definitive conclusion to the narrative.
Foundation's Fear (by Gregory Benford)
Foundation and Chaos (by Greg Bear)
Foundation's Triumph (by David Brin)

Workflow names:
It's tempting but I'd push back a little — the spelled-out names in your current filenames are actually more useful than codes. When you're scanning a file list you immediately know what Flux 2 Klein 9B FP8mixed means. F2K9FP8 requires a decoder in your head.
The only place abbreviations really help is when filenames get long enough to be truncated in File Explorer or the ComfyUI browser. Looking at your current names like 110tb Edit Image - Flux 2 Klein 4B - One Image Edit.json — that's already readable and not excessively long.
If you do standardize anything, I'd focus on consistency rather than abbreviation — you have a few variations like FP8mixed, fp8mixed, FP8 mixed that could be normalized. But that's a minor thing.

title: Spring Cruise — Pre-Cruise Setup Checklist
notes:
GL-SFT1200 + TravelBeast + MacBook

Overview
Everything runs locally — no internet needed. Your software is already working at home. This checklist is purely about verifying the travel hardware works together before you pack it.

1. GL-SFT1200 Router Setup
Admin panel: http://192.168.8.1 — default password: goodlife
Configure your SSID and WPA2 password in Wireless settings. Ignore WAN errors — the WAN port stays empty.
Static DHCP Leases (More Settings → LAN → Static Leases):
DeviceReserved IPTravelBeast (server)192.168.8.10MacBook (client)192.168.8.11
Find each device's Wi-Fi MAC address in OS network settings and bind it to the reserved IP.

2. TravelBeast — Confirm Service Bindings
ComfyUI — launch command must include:
python main.py --listen 0.0.0.0 --port 8188
Ollama — set system environment variable:
OLLAMA_HOST = 0.0.0.0
Restart Ollama after setting it.
Windows Firewall — inbound rules allowing TCP ports 8188 and 11434.

3. End-to-End Test at Home

Power on GL-SFT1200, wait 60 seconds
Connect TravelBeast to GL.iNet Wi-Fi
Start ComfyUI and Ollama on TravelBeast
Connect MacBook to GL.iNet Wi-Fi
Open MacBook browser → http://192.168.8.10:8188
Confirm ComfyUI loads and run a test generation

If it works at home on the GL.iNet network, it works identically in the cabin.

4. Pre-Departure Checklist

 GL.iNet SSID and password configured
 TravelBeast static lease — 192.168.8.10
 MacBook static lease — 192.168.8.11
 ComfyUI --listen 0.0.0.0 flag verified
 OLLAMA_HOST=0.0.0.0 set and Ollama restarted
 Windows Firewall ports 8188 and 11434 open
 End-to-end test completed successfully
 GL.iNet USB-C charger packed
 Ethernet cable packed (for admin panel access)


5. In-Cabin Quick Reference
Router adminhttp://192.168.8.1ComfyUIhttp://192.168.8.10:8188Ollama APIhttp://192.168.8.10:11434TravelBeast IP192.168.8.10MacBook IP192.168.8.11
Startup sequence: GL.iNet on → TravelBeast joins Wi-Fi → start ComfyUI + Ollama → MacBook joins Wi-Fi → open browser → generate.

title: Get new iPhone?
notes:
Storage Upgrade Costs for 512GB ModelsBased on Apple's standard pricing structure (as of September 11, 2025, post-iPhone 17 launch), the cost for the 512GB storage option is an upgrade over the base 256GB for the Pro models and iPhone 17. For the iPhone 16 (base model), the 512GB upgrade is over its base 128GB. The "four cameras" refers to the four models we've discussed: iPhone 17 Pro, iPhone 16 Pro, iPhone 17, and iPhone 16. These are the upgrade costs to get 512GB (i.e., the additional amount added to the base model price for that storage tier). Full model prices would be base + upgrade.Note: iPhone 16 prices reflect estimated post-launch discounts (~$100 off original MSRP for 128GB/256GB equivalents). Actual prices may vary by retailer or carrier promotions—check Apple's site or carriers like Verizon/AT&T for exact deals. No taxes or trade-ins included.

Why iPhone 17 Pro Is BestThe iPhone 17 Pro delivers the best spatial photos/videos for Vision Pro due to:

    Advanced Cameras: 48MP ultrawide with superior low-light performance (vs. iPhone 16 Pro) ensures crisp 3D in dim scenes (e.g., indoor parties). The 48MP 4x telephoto enables sharp zoomed 3D shots (e.g., concerts), unlike the iPhone 17 or 16.
    A19 Pro Chip: Enhances depth mapping and reduces artifacts for lifelike 3D on Vision Pro’s high-res display.
    Audio: Studio-grade mics with advanced Audio Mix create richer Spatial Audio (e.g., directional voices), maximizing immersion.
    Pro Tools: ProRes RAW/Log 2 support advanced editing for 3D content in Final Cut Pro.
    Battery/Cooling: 33 hours and vapor chamber cooling support long shoots without throttling.

Why iPhone 16 Pro Is SecondThe iPhone 16 Pro is a close runner-up:

    Cameras: Matches the iPhone 17 Pro’s 48MP ultrawide for sharp 3D in good light but lags in low-light detail. The 12MP 5x telephoto supports zoomed 3D, though less sharp than the 17 Pro’s 48MP.
    A18 Pro Chip: Solid depth processing but slightly less precise than A19 Pro in complex scenes.
    Value: $100 cheaper ($899) makes it appealing if low-light or zoomed 3D isn’t critical.

Why iPhone 17 and 16 Are “Decent”

    iPhone 17 ($799): Offers “decent” quality with a 48MP ultrawide (sharper than iPhone 16’s 12MP) and A19 chip for good depth, but lacks telephoto for zoomed 3D. Great for well-lit scenes (e.g., family moments).
    iPhone 16 (~$699): Also “decent” but weakest due to 12MP ultrawide, which softens 3D details on Vision Pro. No telephoto, 60Hz display, and shorter battery (22 hours) limit it for enthusiasts.

Recommendation: iPhone 17 Pro ($999)For the best spatial photos and videos, go with the iPhone 17 Pro. Its advanced 48MP ultrawide, 48MP telephoto, A19 Pro chip, and pro editing tools deliver the sharpest, most immersive 3D content for Vision Pro, especially in low light or for zoomed shots. The $100 premium over the iPhone 16 Pro ($899) is worth it for superior low-light performance, sharper zoomed 3D, and future-proofing (e.g., visionOS 26 updates).If the $100 difference is significant, the iPhone 16 Pro is nearly as good in good lighting and offers great value, especially with discounts. For “decent” quality, the iPhone 17 ($799) outperforms the iPhone 16 (~$699) due to its 48MP ultrawide and 120Hz display, but both lag behind the Pro models for top-tier 3D.
